stem: What is the rank of the matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[r31]] & [[r32]] & [[r33]] \end{bmatrix} \)?
solution: Row-reducing shows the third row is the sum of the first two, so it yields no new pivot, while rows 1 and 2 are independent (their \( 2\times2 \) minor \( ([[a]])([[e]])-([[b]])([[d]]) \ne 0 \)). The rank is therefore [[answer]].
distractor_rationales: This counts all three rows as independent, missing that row 3 is a combination of rows 1 and 2.
This under-counts the independent rows.
A nonzero matrix cannot have rank 0.
This is off by one from the correct rank.
This is off by one from the correct rank.
This over-counts the number of pivots.

stem: Every row of \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[r21]] & [[r22]] & [[r23]] \\ [[r31]] & [[r32]] & [[r33]] \end{bmatrix} \) is a scalar multiple of \( ([[a]], [[b]], [[c]]) \). What is the rank of \( A \)?
solution: Rows 2 and 3 are \( [[k1]] \) and \( [[k2]] \) times row 1 respectively, so every row lies along the single direction \( ([[a]], [[b]], [[c]]) \ne (0,0,0) \). Only one row survives row reduction, so the rank is [[answer]].
distractor_rationales: This treats all three rows as independent, ignoring that they are all scalar multiples of each other.
This assumes only one row is dependent, missing that ALL rows are proportional.
A matrix with a nonzero row cannot have rank 0.
This is one of the scaling factors used to build the matrix, not its rank.
This is one of the scaling factors used to build the matrix, not its rank.

stem: None of the rows of \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[g]] & [[h]] & [[i]] \end{bmatrix} \) is an obvious combination of the others. Verify that \( \det(A) \ne 0 \) and give the rank of \( A \).
distractor_rationales: This under-counts the pivots; a nonzero determinant means ALL three rows are independent.
This under-counts the pivots by two.
A matrix with a nonzero determinant cannot have rank 0.
This is off by one from the correct (full) rank.
This is off by two from the correct (full) rank.

stem: What is the rank of the (non-square) matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \end{bmatrix} \)?
solution: A \( 2\times3 \) matrix has rank at most \( 2 \). Since the first two columns form the invertible \( 2\times2 \) block \( \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] \end{bmatrix} \) (determinant \( ([[a]])([[e]])-([[b]])([[d]]) \ne 0 \)), the two rows are independent, so \( A \) achieves the maximum possible rank, [[answer]].
distractor_rationales: The rank of an \(m\times n\) matrix can never exceed \( \min(m,n) \); here that bound is \(2\), not \(3\).
A matrix with a nonzero entry cannot have rank 0.
This is the total number of entries in the matrix, not its rank.
This under-counts the independent rows.
The rank of a \(2\times3\) matrix cannot exceed the number of rows, \(2\).

stem: The matrix \( A = \begin{bmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ 0 & [[d]] & [[e]] \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \) is already in row-echelon form. What is its rank?
solution: The rank of a matrix already in row-echelon form equals its number of nonzero rows. Here rows 1 and 2 have leading pivots \( [[a]] \) and \( [[d]] \), while row 3 is entirely zero, so the rank is [[answer]].
distractor_rationales: This counts the zero row as contributing a pivot, which it cannot.
This misses that TWO rows (not just one) are nonzero.
A matrix with nonzero pivot rows cannot have rank 0.
This is the leading pivot value of row 1, not the count of nonzero rows.
This is the leading pivot value of row 2, not the count of nonzero rows.
